text: costs largely for YouTube and compensation. Total operating expenses were up 24 percent to 28.9

text: tripling year-of-a-year, and operating margin increased from 17.8 percent in the first quarter of last year
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to 32.9 percent. Google cloud's backlog nearly doubles sequentially, reaching 462 billion dollars
speaker: OPERATOR

text: at the end of the first quarter. The increase was driven by strong demand for enterprise AI offerings
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and the inclusion of TPU hardware sales that Sundar referenced earlier. The majority of the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: backlog is related to typical GCP contracts, and we expect to recognize just over 50 percent of
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the backlog as revenue over the next 24 months. In other bets, revenues were $411 million,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and operating loss was $2.1 billion. For the past few years, we have been working to prioritize
speaker: OPERATOR

text: our efforts and investments in the other bets. In Q1 of this year,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Varily completed an external capital raise, the resulted in its de-consolidation from Alphabet.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: G-fiber announced plans to combine with the Stound Broadband, which will result in its de-consolidation
speaker: OPERATOR

text: from Alphabet when the deal closes, which we expect to take place in Q4. And we continue to allocate
speaker: OPERATOR

text: significant resources to businesses where we see meaningful opportunities to create values such as
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Waymo. Turning to our outlook, I would like to provide some commentary on factors that will impact
speaker: OPERATOR

text: our business performance in the second quarter and four-year 2026. First, in terms of revenues,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we're pleased with the overall momentum of the business. At current spot rates, we would expect
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to see an FX tailwind of approximately 1 percentage point to our consolidated revenue in Q2,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: compared to a 3 percentage points FX tailwinds in the first quarter. In Google Cloud, as Sundar
speaker: OPERATOR

text: mentioned, we will begin to deliver a TPU hardware to a select group of customers in their own data
speaker: OPERATOR

text: centers. We expect to begin recognizing a small percent of the revenues from these agreements
speaker: OPERATOR

text: later this year, with the vast majority of revenues to be realized in 2027. It is important to keep
speaker: OPERATOR

text: in mind that revenues from TPU hardware sales will fluctuate from quarter to quarter,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: depending on when TPUs are shipped to customers. And finally, we're excited to welcome the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: WIS team to Google Cloud with the closing of the acquisition in March, and are very pleased with
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the performance to date. A couple of items to highlight related to the acquisition. First,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: WIS will be reported in the Google Cloud segment. And second, we expect a low single digit percentage
speaker: OPERATOR

text: point in headwind to Cloud's operating margin for the remainder of 2026 related to the acquisition.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Moving to investment, we are updating our full year 2026 CapEx Guidance Range
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to 180 to 190 billion, up from our previous estimate of 175 to 185 billion, to now include
speaker: OPERATOR

text: investment related to the acquisition of Intersect, which closed in March. We are seeing unprecedented
speaker: OPERATOR

text: internal and external demand for AI compute resources. The investments we are making in AI
speaker: OPERATOR

text: is delivering strong growth as evidenced by the record revenue and backlog growth in Google Cloud
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and strong performance in Google services. Looking ahead, these strong results reinforce our
speaker: OPERATOR

text: conviction to invest the capital required to continue to capture the AI opportunity.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And the result, we expect our 2027 CapEx to significantly increase compared to 2026.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: In terms of expenses, as we've discussed previously, the significant increase in our
speaker: OPERATOR

text: investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher
speaker: OPERATOR

text: depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: We also expect to continue hiring in key investment areas such as AI and Cloud,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and are investing in marketing to support our AI products. To conclude, Q1 was an outstanding
speaker: OPERATOR

text: quarter for Alphabet, and our teams continue to execute with high level of discipline and velocity
speaker: OPERATOR

text: delivering amazing innovation. We look forward to sharing more in the coming weeks in IO,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Google Marketing Live, and Brandcast. I'm going to take this opportunity to thank our employees
speaker: OPERATOR

text: for their contributions to our performance. Sundar, Philip, and I will now take your questions.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thank you. As a reminder, Taska, question.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: You will need to press star one on your telephone to prevent any background noise. We ask that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you please mute your line once your question has been stated. Your first question comes from
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Brian Novak with Morgan Stanley. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks, Taska. My question is, I have two. The first one, Sundar, on a recent podcast,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you talked about how you were acutely constrained to compute something you focused on
speaker: OPERATOR

text: almost every week to make sure you're deploying capacity correctly. So let me ask you this.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: You look at the search business. What are the areas that you are most excited about applying
speaker: OPERATOR

text: next generation compute toward to generate an ROIC on that return in search in the next 12 months?
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Then the second one is on the sale of the TPUs to third parties. Can you help us
speaker: OPERATOR

text: philosophically understand the strategy around pricing them, given the high ROIC of using TPUs
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to power multi-year Google Cloud workloads a little bit? Thanks.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks, Brian. I'll take the search one first. Obviously, you've seen we are taking advantage
speaker: OPERATOR

text: of all our investments in building the Gemini models and both obviously applying it in search
speaker: OPERATOR

text: in the Gemini app, driving innovations in AI, overviews in AI mode, and they're all contributing
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to the increased usage of the product. I do think looking ahead across both these surfaces,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: there is a massive opportunity to go deeper in what we do for our users. I think bringing
speaker: OPERATOR

text: agent-like flows, workflows to consumers in a way that it's easy for them to do,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: including in the context of search. I see it's a huge opportunity ahead. Obviously,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we are in very early innings of all that, but our investments in our full stack of AI
speaker: OPERATOR

text: approach, I think, puts us in a good position to bring those experiences to search. I'm pretty
speaker: OPERATOR

text: excited about it. On the second question around TPUs, obviously, we do think about it as,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: what are we doing through Google Cloud to help our customers? That's the framework
speaker: OPERATOR

text: with which we think about it. In that context, there are situations where it makes sense. For
speaker: OPERATOR

text: example, you take customers like capital markets where they're running this highly performant
speaker: OPERATOR

text: AI workloads. They wanted TPUs in their data centers. Those trends are true across a diverse
speaker: OPERATOR

text: set of industries. In certain cases, frontier AI labs too. We are opportunistic about it,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: but I do think we step back and think about it overall as the opportunity for Google Cloud.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: A lot of it is providing infrastructure through cloud. At times, it is direct sales of TPU
speaker: OPERATOR

text: hardware to a seller group of customers. But again, we do take our OIC approach,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and some of it helps us get more economies of scale in our overall compute environment as well,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and so helps us invest in the cutting edge, which we need to do the next generation as well.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks, Senator. Your next question comes from Doug Anus with JP Morgan. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks so much for taking the questions. One for a not and one for Philip.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: He talks about 2027 catbacks that it will increase significantly, and I know you didn't quantify,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: but how do you think about the current catbacks trajectory, the ability to service this massive
speaker: OPERATOR

text: backlog that you've built up in just the last quarter and what will no doubt increase going
speaker: OPERATOR

text: forward? And then, Philip, can you just talk more about the drivers of search queries at an all-time
speaker: OPERATOR

text: high, and then how you're thinking about how much room there may be to increase coverage of search
speaker: OPERATOR

text: queries, just the ability to show ads against the higher percentage of queries than the 20%
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you've been at historically. Thanks. Thanks, Doug, for the question. Let me start with your first
speaker: OPERATOR

text: question on the catbacks and how we think about catbacks increase going into 2027.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: You've seen us over the past several years increase catbacks every year, and we have done it very
speaker: OPERATOR

text: thoughtfully to meet the demand that we are seeing, both from external customers as well as demands
speaker: OPERATOR

text: across the organization. And you're seeing the proof point, the ROIC on that, in terms of just a
speaker: OPERATOR

text: growth rate we're seeing, whether it's growth rate within search or certainly the cloud business
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and the opportunity we have within the cloud backlog. So as we're seeing that robust demand
speaker: OPERATOR

text: across the business, we are looking at what can we do to support that grow and demand
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and the opportunity ahead of us. And increasing catbacks, to me, that demand will provide more
speaker: OPERATOR

text: clarity in future earnings call about what that number will be, but that's the opportunity we're
speaker: OPERATOR

text: seeing ahead of us. It's quite meaningful, and we want to make sure we capitalize that and we do
speaker: OPERATOR

text: it in a way that's responsible as we've done to date. So on the second part of your question,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: first of all, just to zoom out for a second, I mean, we're very pleased with the performance
speaker: OPERATOR

text: of our ads business here. And as I'm not shared, Google services benefited from a strong FX tailwind.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: That's important to keep in mind. The strength we saw in search was not due to a single driver,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: but was really the result of many parts of our business showing strength and working very well
speaker: OPERATOR

text: together. If I just deep dive first, second, into the vertical perspective, retail finance,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: I talked about it in health, drove the greatest contribution, although all major verticals actually
speaker: OPERATOR

text: contributed. And we make hundreds of changes every quarter to improve the user experience,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the advertising experience. And so that's really contributing to our performance here. And we've
speaker: OPERATOR

text: also been able to generate very strong ads performance while significantly involving the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: search results page here. The queries continue to grow. And as Sundar mentioned, they're at an all
speaker: OPERATOR

text: time high. We see a overviews and AI mode continue to drive greater search usage and
speaker: OPERATOR

text: growth in overall queries, including in commercial queries. You specifically ask about the 20% on
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the coverage side. And as I said before, I think with the ability of AI to better understand
speaker: OPERATOR

text: intent and a lot of other vectors around it, I think there is upside in that coverage number.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And overall, just the understanding that we have with Gemini on intent has just significantly
speaker: OPERATOR

text: expanded our ability to deliver ads on longer, more complex searches that were previously
speaker: OPERATOR

text: really difficult to monetize. And so I shared earlier, we're deploying our Gemini models now
speaker: OPERATOR

text: across all of our ads infrastructure. And it's really driving improvements across the big three
speaker: OPERATOR

text: areas that are highlighted in my prepared remarks.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thank you, Beth.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Our next question comes from Eric Sheridan with Goldman Sachs. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks so much for taking the questions. Maybe two, if I could. The first one, just building on
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the answer so far when you look at the backlog you disclosed today. So, Nore would love to know if
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you can come back to your comments on AI infrastructure and your unique approach and how that positions
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you to either build capacity, scale, compute, and do it in a way that is, as I said, sort of
speaker: OPERATOR

text: effective from a margin standpoint, as well as a compute standpoint. Just to understand where you
speaker: OPERATOR

text: sit competitively in your mind relative to others. That'd be one. And then Philip, to bring you into
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the conversation, your reference to C.P. And there's been a lot of industry inertia around U.C.P.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: very quickly. Talked with a little bit about what U.C.P. means for this services business as
speaker: OPERATOR

text: agent to commerce scales in the years ahead. Thanks so much.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks, Eric. Look, I do think part of, I mean, I do think we are genuinely differentiated. We
speaker: OPERATOR

text: are unique in the market because of our vertically optimized AI stack and the way we core developed
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the components from our infrastructure and models to platforms and the tools to applications and
speaker: OPERATOR

text: agents. And the fact that we own frontier models, own the silicon, really helps us stay ahead of
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the curve. And on top of it all, just to put an extra point on it, the deep investment in our
speaker: OPERATOR

text: security layers to keep everything safe. And I think we are the only provider in the market that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: offers all of these in a vertical stack. And so overall, again, to my earlier comments to Brian,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: I think about it all as Google Cloud. We have many different ways to serve our customers.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: So we can meet them in a way suited to their needs, I think better than other players here.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And I do think, you know, looking ahead, our ability to invest in this moment and stay at the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: frontier, you know, I think puts us in a strong position. And I think we are doing it based on
speaker: OPERATOR

text: tangible demand signals we are seeing. And it's not just on the revenue side, but I'm talking from
speaker: OPERATOR

text: ROIC framework. And that's what is helping us navigate this moment responsibly.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And to the second part of your question, look, we're in the early stages of the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: agentic era. agentic is more than just completing transactions. We all know this. We see agentic
speaker: OPERATOR

text: experiences as additive and it will really transform how we shop from discovery to decisions while
speaker: OPERATOR

text: helping obviously brands differentiate themselves. We've been very intentional about creating an
speaker: OPERATOR

text: identity experience that works for users or partners for the entire ecosystem. And our goal
speaker: OPERATOR

text: is really to remove the grunt work of shopping. So consumers can focus on the enjoyable parts.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: For decades, you could either shop fast or smart. And I think with the genetic commerce,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: you no longer have to actually choose between speed and certainty here. And the vision is to
speaker: OPERATOR

text: make commercial experiences across the board, assistive, more personal, more fluid. And we're
speaker: OPERATOR

text: carefully designing space and agentic workflows for users to really see valuable components of
speaker: OPERATOR

text: their shopping journey beyond just price, such as customer service, brand loyalty, and more
speaker: OPERATOR

text: while removing the friction of the process that I just talked about. And this is exactly with a
speaker: OPERATOR

text: part of your question kicks in the universal commerce protocol, a new open standard for
speaker: OPERATOR

text: agentic commerce that works actually across the entire shopping journey from the discovery to
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the buying and the post purchase support that we just talked about. And it was really
speaker: OPERATOR

text: co-developed with the industry leaders, including, I mentioned them, Shopify, Etsy, Walmart, and so
speaker: OPERATOR

text: on. And we've received tremendous feedback so far from hundreds of top tech companies,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: payments partners, retailers, really interested in integrating. And it will help power a new
speaker: OPERATOR

text: checkout experience in AI mode, in search, in the Gemini app, and allowing shoppers to actually
speaker: OPERATOR

text: check out from select merchants right as they're researching on Google and going through this
speaker: OPERATOR

text: journey. So we're very, very excited about it.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Our next question comes from Ross Sandler with Barclays. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Yeah, just following up on the last question on agentic shopping. So it seems like we're at
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the point in time where this is actually going to start happening finally. So Phillip, just to
speaker: OPERATOR

text: elaborate a little bit, as you try as you look at carrying the AdWords business from kind of the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: old way of doing things to this new agentic frictionless shopping way, how do you see
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the price and volume kind of growth trends for core AdWords evolving as you start
speaker: OPERATOR

text: implementing more agentic workflows in search?
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Look, our number one focus is obviously on the user experience here. And I think the most important
speaker: OPERATOR

text: part in this is what I mentioned before. We are carefully designing this space in the agentic
speaker: OPERATOR

text: workflows for the users to actually see the valuable components within that shopping journey.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And the second you have the space, you obviously have the ability for interesting app advertising
speaker: OPERATOR

text: models. I think it's also worthwhile noting that beyond just the traditional agents, there is a
speaker: OPERATOR

text: lot of additional ways we can actually use AI to improve the shopping experience. And you can
speaker: OPERATOR

text: think about it like our apparel try-on tools that is now available in the US. You can think about
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Google Lens. So there's a lot more to do here, but I think the key part is actually what I said before.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: We focus on the user experience here. And I think all else will follow if we pay attention to the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: points I mentioned. Your next question comes from Michael Nathanson with Muffet Nathanson.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Your line is now open. Thanks. One percent are one for Philip. So,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: if I can connect Brian's question, Eric's question, and go a little bit higher, I want to
speaker: OPERATOR

text: understand how are you deciding, how are you allocating rich divisions and projects get
speaker: OPERATOR

text: excess capacity even that you're constraining, right? So how do you decide between all the internal
speaker: OPERATOR

text: projects you have and the external projects, right? So what types of screens are you running
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to decide who gets the incremental capacity? And then to Philip, I noticed that he said
speaker: OPERATOR

text: this on the Gemini app. There's more and more images that come to you in the shopping journey.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: You talk about adding advertising on that app and what's guiding your decision-making here
speaker: OPERATOR

text: on adding ads on Gemini. Thanks.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thanks, Michael. I think a great question, you know, on an ongoing basis. I'm looking
speaker: OPERATOR

text: forward to Gemini helping me more and more as I'm thinking that through. Look, I do think that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the foundation where we start with it is, what do we need from a R&D standpoint to develop
speaker: OPERATOR

text: models at the front here? So what do you need for, you know, training these models and so
speaker: OPERATOR

text: effectively the compute needed for GDM because it's a foundation for everything we do. And so
speaker: OPERATOR

text: that's a core principle with which we operate. And then obviously, you know, we, with the ability
speaker: OPERATOR

text: to plan ahead, we are, we do, you know, we do long range plans on our core areas, be it,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: be it search, be it YouTube and so on, as well as what we see in Google Cloud. And obviously,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: in Google Cloud, you know, we have, we are providing enterprise AI solutions, which, you know,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: which, you know, this quarter, you know, had an 800% year-on-year increase from the prior year.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: So we're seeing strong demand for Gemini enterprise, our AI solutions there. We see
speaker: OPERATOR

text: strong demand for infrastructure in Google Cloud. And as I said earlier, in some cases,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we are seeing demand for TPU hardware, TPU hardware and others data centers as well.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: So, you know, we are, we are modeling these out and working to allocate across these areas.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Obviously, we are compute constraint in the, in the near term. And as an example, our cloud
speaker: OPERATOR

text: revenue would have been higher if you were able to meet the demand. So we are working through that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: moment. And, you know, we are investing, but we have a robust, you know, long range planning
speaker: OPERATOR

text: framework. And, you know, we see extraordinary opportunities ahead. And, you know, we are
speaker: OPERATOR

text: allocating with that framework in mind. And to the second part of your question, as I said,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: in my previous answer, we were obviously focused on the user first and creating a really great
speaker: OPERATOR

text: user experience with all of our product, especially on neural products. And specifically on monetization
speaker: OPERATOR

text: in the Gemini app, our focus right now is on AI mode. But it's fair to say that we really believe
speaker: OPERATOR

text: a format that works well in AI mode would transfer successfully to Gemini app. And so,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: today in the Gemini app, we're focused on the free tier and subscriptions and our AI plans
speaker: OPERATOR

text: were a sizable contributor to our Google One revenue growth. But let's also be clear ads have
speaker: OPERATOR

text: always been a big part of skidding products to reach billions of people. And if done well,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: ads can be really valuable and really helpful commercial information. And at the right moment,

text: we'll share any plans as we have said, but we're not rushing anything here.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Your next question comes from Mark Schmullick with Alliance Bernstein. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Yes, thanks for taking the question. Philip, one more on search performance if I can.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: You talked a few times about kind of optimizing for the consumer experience. And I guess,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: besides higher query volume, it's fair to conclude that consumers are using these AI tools,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Googles are otherwise, and it's shrinking their purchasing journeys, converting at higher rates.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: And if so, is there a way to dimensionalize how much of the strength and search is being driven
speaker: OPERATOR

text: by that behavioral change? Again, perhaps some of the newer advertiser AI tools that you've been
speaker: OPERATOR

text: launching and rolling out. Thank you. I think the way to think about it is really to think about
speaker: OPERATOR

text: the expansionary moment we see here for search. This is the key part. AI is fundamentally changing
speaker: OPERATOR

text: how the world searches for and how it accesses information. Chores are at an all time high,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: so understand this. Traditional search really started with 10 blue links. And now we have
speaker: OPERATOR

text: overviews in AI mode, and they have made search more intelligent than ever, and they let you ask
speaker: OPERATOR

text: far more complex questions. And we have lens or circle to search, and we have search live.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Search live is now available to all countries and languages that support AI mode. Again,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: it shows you the expansionary nature of it. And we have our AI driven search campaigns,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: and we have now SMBs that can reach customers at a scale that it really wasn't possible even
speaker: OPERATOR

text: a few years ago. And you can add in Google Translate and so on. So I feel if you factor all of this in,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we're in a pretty good place and are quite excited about where this is going.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Your next question comes from Ron Josi-Witt City. Your line is now open.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Great. Thanks for taking the question. Maybe this one is for or not. With margins continue to
speaker: OPERATOR

text: expand here. I wanted to understand maybe if you could break down the cost drivers or really the
speaker: OPERATOR

text: drivers of margin expansion, particularly amongst cloud. There's a piece of that that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: AI revenues are lower margin in general, but we are seeing margins improve. So more insight
speaker: OPERATOR

text: on just the cloud business and what's driving that margin expansion. Obviously demand may be
speaker: OPERATOR

text: pricing, but that would be helpful. Thank you. Sure. Let me help unpack the margin expansion.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Obviously, we're pleased to see that there are pushes and pools across the business,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: including within cloud specifically. And I would start with the top line. When we see this robust,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: strong revenue growth, both in cloud and Google services, it does provide leverage all the way
speaker: OPERATOR

text: down to the bottom line within the income statement. And you know, we've been working hard to ensure
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we're running a productive and efficient organization. And it's not just how we operate the business,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: but even in areas such as our technical infrastructure, where we are investing these significant
speaker: OPERATOR

text: capex investments in our data centers and servers, we are looking at how we drive
speaker: OPERATOR

text: scientific process innovation within that organization. And that is reflected both in cloud and Google
speaker: OPERATOR

text: services as we allocate cost based on consumption. In the past, I did talk about the depreciation
speaker: OPERATOR

text: associate with these investments that is hitting both Google Cloud and Google services. Google
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Cloud expanded margin quite significantly from a year ago, as you've seen in our numbers that we've
speaker: OPERATOR

text: just previewed. And a lot of it is the top line growth that Google Cloud is providing or producing,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: as well as an incredibly efficient way of running the business. I will give Thomas and the team a
speaker: OPERATOR

text: lot of credit for running a very productive organization and making sure that we are supporting
speaker: OPERATOR

text: our customers and providing the services and products that they want and benefit from,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: continue to drive top line growth and doing this well within the middle of the income statement,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: all the way from a very efficient technical infrastructure, thinking through how do we leverage
speaker: OPERATOR

text: AI across our business? As Sundar mentioned, the use of coding internally or how Gemini helps us
speaker: OPERATOR

text: there, optimizing our real estate footprint. And we're going to continue to do this. This is not,
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we're not going to stop here. We're going to continue to push for more efficiency knowing that
speaker: OPERATOR

text: we're going to have the headwind associate with a depreciation coming with higher capex level.
speaker: OPERATOR

text: Thank you. Very helpful. Our next question comes from Ken Gerelske with Wells Fargo. Your line is
speaker: OPERATOR

text: now open. Thank you very much. Two of them I made, please. First, on the cloud and capacity,
speaker: OPERATOR
